sources.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology (Parker, Schneegurt, Tu, Lister, Forster)
sources.note: Multiple-choice Review Questions scraped verbatim; correctAnswer taken from OpenStax published Answer Key pages (pages/chapter-N). OpenStax posts a 'no LLM training' notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing per PRD, with attribution and non-commercial/share-alike compliance.

meta.notes: File 01 batch A: OpenStax Microbiology Ch. 3 (The Cell). 'Select all that apply' and >4-option items were skipped. Option numbers 1-4 map to labels A-D; correctAnswer letters verified against the chapter answer key.

questions.stem: Which of the following individuals argued in favor of the theory of spontaneous generation?
questions.explanation: John Needham supported spontaneous generation, arguing that microbial growth appearing in boiled broth showed life arising from nonliving matter. Redi, Spallanzani, and later Pasteur produced evidence against it.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q1 = C.

hints.prompt: What does the theory of spontaneous generation claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Living organisms can arise directly from nonliving matter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Living cells arise only from pre-existing cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Species change over time through natural selection

hints.rationale: Spontaneous generation is the idea that life springs directly from nonliving material.

hints.prompt: A supporter of spontaneous generation would have pointed to which kind of experimental result as evidence for their view?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microbial growth appearing in broth that had been boiled

choices.label: C
choices.text: Broth in a swan-neck flask staying clear and sterile

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fewer infections after doctors washed their hands

hints.rationale: A proponent cited apparent growth in previously boiled broth as life arising from nonliving matter.

hints.prompt: Which pair of scientists is famous for producing evidence AGAINST spontaneous generation, and so can be ruled out as the proponent asked for here?
hints.rationale: Redi and Pasteur are classic opponents of spontaneous generation, so neither is the proponent.

questions.stem: Which of the following individuals is credited for definitively refuting the theory of spontaneous generation using broth in a swan-neck flask?
questions.explanation: Louis Pasteur's swan-neck flask experiments showed that broth remained sterile while air (but not dust/microbes) could reach it, definitively refuting spontaneous generation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q2 = D.

hints.prompt: In a swan-neck (S-curved) flask experiment, what does the curved neck accomplish?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It lets air reach the broth while trapping dust and microbes in the bend

choices.label: B
choices.text: It seals the flask completely so no air can enter

choices.label: D
choices.text: It slowly adds fresh nutrients to the broth

hints.rationale: The bend admits air but traps airborne microbes, so sterile broth stays sterile.

hints.prompt: Because the broth in the swan-neck flask stayed sterile even though air still reached it, what did the experiment demonstrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microbes come from pre-existing microbes carried in air/dust, not from the broth itself

choices.label: B
choices.text: Air by itself spontaneously generates microbes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Broth cannot support microbial growth under any conditions

hints.rationale: Contamination required airborne microbes, refuting spontaneous generation.

hints.prompt: Which pair historically argued FOR spontaneous generation, so neither could be the scientist who refuted it with the swan-neck flask?
hints.rationale: Aristotle and Needham both supported spontaneous generation, so neither refuted it.

questions.stem: Which of the following scientists experimented with raw meat, maggots, and flies in an attempt to disprove the theory of spontaneous generation?
questions.explanation: Francesco Redi covered some jars of meat and left others open, showing maggots only appeared where flies could lay eggs — evidence against spontaneous generation of maggots.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q3 = D.

hints.prompt: An experiment that covers some meat and leaves other meat exposed to flies is designed to test which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether maggots appear only when flies can reach the meat to lay eggs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether heat kills microbes in broth

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether air is required for life

hints.rationale: Covering meat blocks flies, testing whether maggots need fly eggs rather than arising on their own.

hints.prompt: If maggots appear only on the meat that flies could reach, what does that show?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maggots develop from eggs laid by flies, not from the meat itself

choices.label: C
choices.text: Flies form from maggots without any eggs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Maggots require direct sunlight to form

hints.rationale: Maggots came from fly eggs, disproving their spontaneous generation.

hints.prompt: Which two of the four listed individuals did NOT perform experiments against spontaneous generation (so they can be ruled out)?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lazzaro Spallanzani and Antonie van Leeuwenhoek

hints.rationale: Aristotle proposed spontaneous generation and van Leeuwenhoek is known for microscopy; neither ran experiments against it, whereas Redi and Spallanzani did.

questions.stem: Which of the following individuals did not contribute to the establishment of cell theory?
questions.explanation: Girolamo Fracastoro proposed an early germ theory of contagion, not cell theory. Schleiden, Remak, and Hooke all contributed to the development of cell theory.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q4 = A.

hints.prompt: Cell theory is the principle that states which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All living things are made of cells, which arise from pre-existing cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diseases are spread by contagious agents

hints.rationale: Cell theory holds that cells are the basic unit of life and come from earlier cells.

hints.prompt: To find the person who did NOT help establish cell theory, each scientist's work should be checked against which criterion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether their work concerns cells as the fundamental unit of life

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether they ever used a microscope

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether they lived and worked in Europe

hints.rationale: A cell-theory contribution must specifically concern cells as the unit of life; the odd one out worked on something else.

hints.prompt: Which two of the listed scientists are firmly associated with founding cell theory and therefore can be ruled out as the answer?
hints.rationale: Schleiden (plants are made of cells) and Hooke (coined the term 'cell') are core cell-theory figures, so neither is the odd one out.

questions.stem: Whose proposal of the endosymbiotic theory of mitochondrial and chloroplast origin was ultimately accepted by the greater scientific community?
questions.explanation: Lynn Margulis championed the endosymbiotic theory, which holds that mitochondria and chloroplasts arose from once free-living prokaryotes engulfed by an ancestral eukaryotic cell.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: The endosymbiotic theory proposes that mitochondria and chloroplasts originated from which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Once free-living prokaryotes engulfed by an ancestral eukaryotic cell

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infoldings of the host cell's nuclear membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: Viruses that permanently integrated into the genome

choices.label: D
choices.text: Crystallized proteins that formed in the cytoplasm

hints.rationale: The theory holds these organelles descend from free-living bacteria taken up by a host cell.

hints.prompt: Which observation most strongly supports an endosymbiotic (bacterial) origin for mitochondria and chloroplasts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They contain their own circular DNA and bacteria-like ribosomes

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are surrounded by only a single membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are found only in prokaryotic cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: They contain no genetic material of their own

hints.rationale: Their own DNA and prokaryote-like ribosomes point to a free-living bacterial ancestry.

hints.prompt: Which two of the listed scientists are famous for work OTHER than the origin of organelles and can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ignaz Semmelweis (hand hygiene) and Theodor Schwann (animal cell theory)

hints.rationale: Semmelweis (handwashing) and Schwann (cell theory) are unrelated to the origin of organelles.

questions.stem: Which of the following developed a set of postulates for determining whether a particular disease is caused by a particular pathogen?
questions.explanation: Robert Koch formulated Koch's postulates, the criteria used to establish a causal link between a specific microorganism and a specific disease.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q6 = B.

hints.prompt: A set of postulates linking a specific microbe to a specific disease is used to establish which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A causal relationship between one pathogen and one disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: The correct dose of an antibiotic

choices.label: C
choices.text: The geographic spread of an outbreak

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sterilization time for surgical instruments

hints.rationale: Such postulates are criteria for proving that a microbe causes a disease.

hints.prompt: Which requirement is central to these disease-causation postulates?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The microbe is isolated from a sick host and shown to cause the same disease in a healthy host

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient recovers after doctors wash their hands

choices.label: C
choices.text: Removing a contaminated water pump stops an outbreak

choices.label: D
choices.text: Treating surgical tools with carbolic acid prevents infection

hints.rationale: Isolating the organism and reproducing the disease is the heart of the postulates.

hints.prompt: Which two of the listed people are known for achievements OTHER than formal disease-causation postulates?

choices.label: A
choices.text: John Snow (cholera epidemiology) and Joseph Lister (antiseptic surgery)

hints.rationale: Snow mapped a cholera outbreak and Lister pioneered antiseptics; neither authored the postulates.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms refers to a prokaryotic cell that is comma shaped?
questions.explanation: A vibrio is a short, curved (comma-shaped) rod. Cocci are spherical, coccobacilli are short rods, and spirilla are rigid spirals.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: A 'comma-shaped' bacterial cell is best described as which of the following?
hints.rationale: A comma shape is a short, gently curved rod.

hints.prompt: How does a comma-shaped (curved-rod) cell differ from a spiral-shaped cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A curved rod has a single short bend, whereas a spiral has multiple twists

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are exactly the same shape

choices.label: C
choices.text: A curved rod is spherical while a spiral is a straight rod

choices.label: D
choices.text: A curved rod is always much larger than a spiral

hints.rationale: A comma/curved rod has one bend, while spirilla have several helical turns.

hints.prompt: Which two shapes have NO curvature at all, so they can be eliminated for a comma shape?

choices.label: A
choices.text: coccus (a sphere) and coccobacillus (a short straight rod)

hints.rationale: A sphere and a straight rod have no curve, unlike comma or spiral shapes.

questions.stem: Which of the following cell wall components is unique to gram-negative cells?
questions.explanation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is found in the outer membrane of gram-negative bacteria. Teichoic acid is characteristic of gram-positive walls, mycolic acid of acid-fast cells, and peptidoglycan is found in both.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q9 = A.

hints.prompt: Which structural feature is characteristic of gram-negative bacteria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An outer membrane lying outside a thin peptidoglycan layer

choices.label: B
choices.text: A single thick peptidoglycan layer with no outer membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: A waxy wall rich in mycolic acid

hints.rationale: Gram-negative cells uniquely have an outer membrane over a thin peptidoglycan layer.

hints.prompt: A component described as 'unique to gram-negative cells' would most likely be located where?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In the outer membrane that only gram-negative cells possess

choices.label: B
choices.text: In the thick wall of gram-positive cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: In the waxy layer of acid-fast cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: In a structure shared by all bacteria

hints.rationale: A gram-negative-specific molecule resides in their distinctive outer membrane.

hints.prompt: Peptidoglycan is a tempting choice. Why is it NOT the 'unique to gram-negative' component?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Peptidoglycan is present in essentially all bacterial walls, both gram-negative and gram-positive

choices.label: B
choices.text: Peptidoglycan is found only in gram-negative cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peptidoglycan is a lipid located in the outer membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: Peptidoglycan is never part of any cell wall

hints.rationale: Because peptidoglycan is shared by both wall types, it cannot be the gram-negative-unique component.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms refers to a bacterial cell having a single tuft of flagella at one end?
questions.explanation: Lophotrichous bacteria have a tuft (cluster) of multiple flagella at one pole. Monotrichous = single flagellum, amphitrichous = flagella at both ends, peritrichous = flagella all over.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q10 = D.

hints.prompt: In bacterial flagella terminology, what does a 'tuft' of flagella refer to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A cluster of multiple flagella grouped together

choices.label: C
choices.text: Flagella spread evenly over the whole surface

hints.rationale: A tuft is a cluster of several flagella.

hints.prompt: A 'single tuft at one end' means the flagella are arranged how?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Several flagella clustered together at one pole

choices.label: B
choices.text: One flagellum at a single pole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Flagella at both poles of the cell

choices.label: D
choices.text: Flagella scattered over the entire cell

hints.rationale: A tuft at one end means multiple flagella at a single pole.

hints.prompt: Which two arrangements place flagella at MORE than one location (so they are not a single-end tuft) and can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: amphitrichous (flagella at both poles) and peritrichous (flagella all over)

hints.rationale: Both place flagella in multiple locations rather than as one tuft at a single end.

questions.stem: Bacterial cell walls are primarily composed of which of the following?
questions.explanation: The bacterial cell wall is built mainly of peptidoglycan (murein), a polymer of sugar chains cross-linked by short peptides.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: The bacterial cell wall's main polymer must be strong enough to resist osmotic pressure. What class of molecule forms this rigid mesh?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A cross-linked polymer of sugar chains and short peptides

hints.rationale: A rigid wall is built from a cross-linked sugar–peptide mesh.

hints.prompt: How does the bacterial cell WALL differ in composition from the plasma MEMBRANE beneath it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The wall is a rigid sugar–peptide mesh, while the membrane is a phospholipid bilayer

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both the wall and the membrane are phospholipid bilayers

choices.label: C
choices.text: The wall is made of DNA and the membrane of protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: The wall is pure carbohydrate and the membrane is pure protein

hints.rationale: The wall is a sugar–peptide polymer; the membrane is a lipid bilayer.

hints.prompt: Which two options are clearly NOT the wall's primary polymer — one being the main lipid of the membrane, the other the class of molecules that act as enzymes and motors?
hints.rationale: Phospholipids build the membrane and proteins act as enzymes/motors; neither is the wall's primary polymer.

questions.stem: Which of the following organelles is not part of the endomembrane system?
questions.explanation: The endomembrane system includes the nuclear envelope, ER, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, vesicles, and plasma membrane. Peroxisomes are generally not considered part of it.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q12 = D.

hints.prompt: The endomembrane system is defined as a set of membranous organelles connected by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct membrane continuity or transfer of membrane by vesicles

hints.rationale: Endomembrane organelles are linked by membrane continuity and vesicular traffic.

hints.prompt: Three of the four listed organelles are classic vesicle-trafficking members. Which trait sets the odd one apart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It carries out oxidative reactions and is not fed by the ER–Golgi vesicle pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: It stores the cell's chromosomal DNA

hints.rationale: The outlier is an oxidative organelle not supplied by ER–Golgi vesicle flow.

hints.prompt: Which two organelles are indisputably part of the vesicle-trafficking secretory pathway, so cannot be the exception?
hints.rationale: The ER and Golgi are core members of the secretory pathway, so neither is the odd one out.

questions.stem: Which type of cytoskeletal fiber is important in the formation of the nuclear lamina?
questions.explanation: The nuclear lamina is a meshwork of intermediate filaments (lamins) lining the inner nuclear membrane.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: The nuclear lamina is a mesh lining the inner nuclear membrane. Its main structural role is to do which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide mechanical support and shape to the nuclear envelope

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transport vesicles to the Golgi apparatus

hints.rationale: The lamina mechanically supports and shapes the nucleus.

hints.prompt: The lamina's lamin proteins are stable, rope-like fibers specialized for bearing mechanical tension (not for rapid assembly or motor transport). Which property matches the needed fiber?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dynamic hollow tracks for motor-driven transport

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thin fibers that drive cell crawling and cytokinesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secreted fibers found outside the cell

hints.rationale: The lamina needs durable tension-bearing fibers, not transport tracks or motility fibers.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated — one being an extracellular matrix protein (not cytoskeletal at all), the other being the hollow tubes used as transport tracks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: fibronectin (extracellular matrix) and microtubules (hollow transport tracks)

hints.rationale: Fibronectin is extracellular (not cytoskeletal) and microtubules are hollow transport tracks; neither builds the lamina.

questions.stem: Sugar groups may be added to proteins in which of the following?
questions.explanation: Glycosylation — the addition of sugar groups to proteins — is completed in the Golgi apparatus (with initial steps in the rough ER).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q14 = C.

hints.prompt: Glycosylation is which of the following processes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The addition of sugar (carbohydrate) groups to proteins

choices.label: B
choices.text: The removal of water from a protein

choices.label: C
choices.text: The folding of a protein by chaperones

choices.label: D
choices.text: The breakdown of a protein into amino acids

hints.rationale: Glycosylation is the attachment of sugar groups to proteins.

hints.prompt: In the secretory pathway, which compartment is the main site for FINISHING carbohydrate modifications and sorting proteins for shipment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The organelle that processes and packages proteins after they leave the ER

choices.label: B
choices.text: The organelle that mainly makes lipids and detoxifies drugs

choices.label: C
choices.text: The digestive organelle full of hydrolytic enzymes

choices.label: D
choices.text: The organelle that houses the cell's DNA

hints.rationale: Completing glycosylation and packaging proteins occurs after the ER, in the Golgi.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated — one specializing in lipid synthesis/detox, the other in hydrolytic digestion?

choices.label: B
choices.text: rough endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi apparatus

choices.label: C
choices.text: smooth endoplasmic reticulum and rough endoplasmic reticulum

hints.rationale: The smooth ER makes lipids/detoxifies and the lysosome digests; neither completes protein glycosylation.

questions.stem: Which of the following structures of a eukaryotic cell is not likely derived from an endosymbiotic bacterium?
questions.explanation: Under the endosymbiotic theory, the mitochondrion's outer membrane comes from the host cell's membrane (formed during engulfment), while the DNA, ribosomes, and inner membrane derive from the ancestral bacterium.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: Under endosymbiotic theory, features inherited from the ancestral free-living bacterium would most resemble which of the following?
hints.rationale: Bacterium-derived features are prokaryote-like: their own DNA, ribosomes, and original membrane.

hints.prompt: Why does a mitochondrion have two membranes under the endosymbiotic model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One membrane is the original bacterial membrane; the other was added by the host during engulfment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both membranes were part of the free-living bacterium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both membranes came from the host cell's nucleus

choices.label: D
choices.text: The membranes formed spontaneously from free lipids in the cytosol

hints.rationale: One membrane is bacterial in origin; the other is host-derived from engulfment.

hints.prompt: Which two mitochondrial components are the clearest evidence of a bacterial ancestor (and thus ARE bacterium-derived, ruling them out)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: the inner membrane and the outer membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: mitochondrial DNA and the outer membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: mitochondrial ribosomes and the outer membrane

hints.rationale: Its own DNA and its own ribosomes are hallmark bacterial features, so both are bacterium-derived.

questions.stem: Which type of nutrient uptake involves the engulfment of small dissolved molecules into vesicles?
questions.explanation: Pinocytosis ('cell drinking') nonspecifically engulfs extracellular fluid and small dissolved solutes into vesicles.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q16 = B.

hints.prompt: Bringing extracellular material into the cell by wrapping it in a piece of membrane to form a vesicle is called which of the following?
hints.rationale: Vesicle-based uptake of external material is endocytosis.

hints.prompt: The stem specifies NONspecific uptake of small DISSOLVED molecules and fluid. Which description matches that?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nonspecific gulping of extracellular fluid and its dissolved solutes into vesicles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Receptor-specific capture of one target molecule into vesicles

choices.label: C
choices.text: Engulfing large solid particles such as whole bacteria

choices.label: D
choices.text: Moving ions through a channel protein without any vesicle

hints.rationale: Nonspecific fluid and solute uptake in vesicles is 'cell drinking.'

hints.prompt: Which two options move solutes through membrane PROTEINS (with no vesicle formation) and can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Active transport and facilitated diffusion use carrier/channel proteins, not vesicles.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not composed of microtubules?
questions.explanation: Desmosomes are cell-cell anchoring junctions linked to intermediate filaments. Centrioles, eukaryotic flagella, and cilia are all built from microtubules.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 3 The Cell — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-3), Ch.3 MC Q17 = A.

hints.prompt: Microtubules are hollow tubes of tubulin. A structure 'made of microtubules' would be involved in which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cell movement/beating and organizing chromosomes during division

choices.label: C
choices.text: Forming the lipid barrier of the cell

hints.rationale: Microtubule structures drive motility (cilia/flagella) and organize division (centrioles).

hints.prompt: Three of the four listed structures are motility or organizing structures. The exception instead does which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anchors adjacent cells together and links to intermediate filaments

choices.label: B
choices.text: Beats to move fluid across the cell surface

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whips back and forth to propel the whole cell

choices.label: D
choices.text: Forms the poles that organize the mitotic spindle

hints.rationale: The exception is a cell-adhesion junction tied to intermediate filaments, not microtubules.

hints.prompt: Which two structures are unmistakably built from a microtubule core, so neither can be the exception?
hints.rationale: Cilia and flagella are built from a microtubule axoneme, so both are microtubule structures.
